speaker: interviewer
text: Zabielak. We're in London, England, and this is tape number six/ You were saying that your husband became sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband became sick. He had a cut to the artery, which sent an embolism to the brain. The brain got clogged up. And on the 7th of May, 1973, he passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was quite a lot older than you, wasn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And Sylvia [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was coming here every week, nearly, with the husband and the child. And I used to go there. And we were on very good terms. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In 1976-- '75, Sylvia [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went on holiday, and then she had a lump under the breast. They operated. They said it is malignant, but she will be all right. I went with Henry to Israel for Pesach. When I came back, Sylvia came to me, and she said-- because it was the anniversary of the death of my husband, and she came that we should go together to the cemetery. And she came, and she told me that she-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that she got something on her womb, and she's going into hospital to be operated, to the Royal Free.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was her little boy at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight. And two months later, she passed away. And I tried with all my strength to keep him, but I had complications with Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Henry, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: 25. Henry fell in love with a woman who was married before and had three children. I was very much against that match, that all the time, I used to run away from home. I thought he would leave. And two years later-- if now it's 18 years, then I don't know what it was, the day-- I was in Israel for the holidays, and he rang me up that he's getting married. I had a cousin in Israel, and she said, you don't want to lose your son. You will gain a family. You go and bless them for their marriage. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And Henry got married with the wife, and she had three children. It was very difficult in the beginning, but now they are very happy. And I am now a great-grandmother, thank God. My daughter-in-law got three grandchildren. They are my great-grandchildren. And the best day of my life was on the 22nd of May, 1979, when my grandson was born. And ever since, my life changed, because I looked after my grandson. I tried to look after him and to do everything what I can. Now, I-- you see, I was the chairman of [NON-ENGLISH]. And I always tried, and I ran a big business with property, and so on. I closed the business. And now I am retired, and I try to help other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is your grandson now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson will be 17 this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you have a message to leave to future generations about the Holocaust, what would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you what I would say-- that they are very happy. They should be very happy that they've got the State of Israel. And they've got to look for it, because it can always happen another thing. But as long as we've got the State of Israel, a thing like this can't happen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I think they should live with other people, not just with Jewish people, but with other people as well, and show them an example what it was and what passed by. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my maternal aunt, Regina, and my husband. She was his first wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was taken in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was taken in Poland, I think before they got married. This is their daughter, Sylvia, during the war, when she left the ghetto, and she was by the Polish woman which was good to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she became your step-daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she became my stepdaughter later. This is in 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Lodz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It is Krisha and Rishek, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my very dear friends. They live now in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am this one with the gray hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The lighter hat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was taken outside the Kinloss Gardens Synagogue on the 30th of March, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me and Arnold, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your wedding day?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my wedding. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my son, Henry, eight days old. It was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 10th of May, 1948. He was born on the 2nd of May, '48. This is Henry, eight months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in London. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is Sylvia in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and myself on Delphi's wedding, on June 1949. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is Henry's bar mitzvah on the 16th of May, 1961. My husband, Arnold, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at Selby's in Hanover Square in London. This is Ron, Sylvia's husband. Then it's Sylvia, my husband, Henry, myself, my mother, Uncle Markosfeld. My cousin Yirka, and her daughter, Ilana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at Selby's on the 16th of May, 1961.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Henry's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Henry's bar mitzvah. This is Anthony, my grandson, eight days old, with me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My grandson, Anthony, 10 years old. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Henry and Anthony on Anthony's bar mitzvah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anthony's bar mitzvah. It is in Carlton Towers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It is in June-- I don't remember the date-- in June '93.

speaker: interviewee
text: '92, Yes. Anthony's bar mitzvah, it is Anthony, my daughter-in-law, Vivian, me, and Henry. Here is Anthony, presented by the Lord Mayor with a scroll, giving him a scholarship of 2,000 pounds for the next two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the Lord  Mayor of London?

speaker: interviewee
text: London. Amanda and Mark Gershenson. She's the daughter of my daughter-in-law. Steven and Deborah [NON-ENGLISH], the second daughter of my daughter-in-law. Mark Gershenson, Vivian Arnold, my daughter-in-law, Henry Arnold, my son, Deborah-- the bride-- and Steven [NON-ENGLISH], Amanda Gershenson-- the daughter of my daughter-in-law-- Anthony, my grandson, and Madeline. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was the wedding of Deborah in Claridge's, London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter-in-law, Vivian Arnold, my son, Henry Arnold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandson, Anthony.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can I ask Henry first, do you think your life would have been different if your parents hadn't been Holocaust survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. I think my attitudes to life would be very different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was was brought up that every bedtime story was a story about my parents' experiences during the war. And it certainly gave me a feeling that I didn't wish to go anywhere near Germany, or anywhere near Poland, or whatever. And certainly, in the last few years, where I've perhaps wanted to go and see things in Poland, even till today, my mother tries to prevent all that. And, on the other hand, I feel that we can't live forever hating the Germans. I understand that they've had a terrible effect upon my mother and father's life. But you can't pursue that from generation to generation to generation. And I think you have to be friendly with them, and you have to explain what happened. Because I remember one evening, we went out with my mother and some people that we know from Germany. And they had never heard any of the type of stories-- because they are my age and my wife's age-- of my mother's experiences. And when my mother started to relate these stories, the people started-- the woman started crying. And all I can say to you is, I actually believe that more would be achieved now by a dialogue with Germans than by ignoring them. That's really the way I've-- the effect it's had on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't want him to go and find out about his heritage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't want to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you something, lately, I thought perhaps I would go just to see the grave of my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe your son will go with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she'd be too upset to go. I think it would be a terrible experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I mean, a lot of people I've interviewed have been, and it has been incredibly traumatic. But they've felt some release--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: --Having been. So it's a very personal thing. How do you find having a grandmother who's been through all that she's been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: It makes a big difference. You get to have a different perspective on life. When you've looked at what other people have gone through, you appreciate what you've got all the more. And you see a different side of life. And you realize what a different sort of life you have to many other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you discuss this sort of thing with other of your friends who've got grandparents who maybe haven't had the same experiences? Do you find that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, occasionally, and they relate quite well, I think. But you can tell the difference. People do have different attitudes to certain things, people that have heard about it and often come into contact with people like that, but yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I certainly, know-- the way you said that, I remember-- now, I've got one friend whose parents were in Hungary and Germany during the war. And, interestingly enough, I relate to him better than to any of my friends whose parents were from England during the war. We can both-- we can talk to each other, and we almost know what the other one's answer is going to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that's very significant, because I think that's something that comes out with lots of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think our attitude to life is totally different to those of our contemporaries whose parents did not go through this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything you'd like to add? I don't want to leave you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, only just to say that my parents were both born in England. And my father was in the British Army, and he will never speak about any of his experiences. I know he was in Germany when some of the concentration camps were liberated, and whether he actually saw anything-- but when I was a child, my parents basically kept it all from me. And I don't think I knew-- really, I don't remember learning anything about it until I was about 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was he involved with the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He won't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even now. He doesn't want to talk about anything to do with that. And even, now he won't. And he just says, that he was in the army. And he'll talk about things he did when he was in the army. But I asked him once, and he just said, I would never talk about anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that any of you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. I think that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we've learned a lot. I think I've learned a lot, and my children have learned a lot from knowing Rena and some of her friends, and hearing their experiences and what they went through. And I've just come back from America. I went to the new Holocaust Museum in Washington. And at the end of the museum, you get to sit in a room, and you hear a lot of survivors talking about their experiences, and how they coped after the war. And a  friend I was with was crying her eyes out. But I wasn't, because I felt, you know, that I've been with Rena, and I've seen so many of her friends. And I just felt it was like just more people who'd been through these terrible things. And how they ever survived and how they're normal or walking around today or leading a normal life, I cannot imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.
